problems.title: Build Adjacency List from Edge List
problems.note: Convert edge list [(u,v)] into dict/array of neighbor lists. Directed vs undirected variants. O(V+E) space. Must be automatic — all graph problems start here. Prerequisite for Find if Path Exists (34), Clone Graph (16), and every BFS/DFS problem.

problems.title: BFS on a Graph (Iterative)
problems.note: Enqueue source, mark visited, process level-by-level. O(V+E). Prerequisite for Rotting Oranges (11), Snake & Ladder (21), Open the Lock (26), and every shortest-path-in-unweighted-graph problem. BFS = shortest path when all edges have equal weight.

problems.title: DFS on a Graph (Recursive + Iterative)
problems.note: Recursive: mark visited, recurse on unvisited neighbors. Iterative: explicit stack. O(V+E). Prerequisite for All Paths From Source (45), Clone Graph (16), Cycle Detection, and all DFS-based problems. Must know both forms.

problems.title: Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph (DFS + BFS)
problems.note: DFS: if a visited neighbor is not the direct parent, cycle exists. BFS: same via parent tracking. O(V+E). Prerequisite for Graph Valid Tree (23) and Redundant Connection (18). Critical distinction: parent node is not a back-edge in undirected graphs.

problems.title: Detect Cycle in Directed Graph (DFS Color / Kahn's)
problems.note: DFS with 3-color states (white/gray/black): gray neighbor = back-edge = cycle. Or Kahn's BFS: if not all nodes dequeued, cycle exists. O(V+E). Prerequisite for Course Schedule II (6), Alien Dictionary (2), Find Eventual Safe States (22).

problems.title: Cheapest Flights (K Stops)
problems.note: Entry point for constrained shortest path. Bellman-Ford with K-step limit. Prerequisite for Cheapest Flights Within K Stops (215) and all Dijkstra variants. Must understand state = (node, stops_remaining).

problems.title: Reconstruct Itinerary
problems.note: Hierholzer's algorithm for Eulerian path. Key insight: use a min-heap adjacency list and post-order DFS. Prerequisite for all Eulerian circuit / path problems. Tricky edge case: must use all edges exactly once.

problems.title: Find if Path Exists
problems.note: Simplest graph reachability problem. Solvable via BFS, DFS, or Union-Find. Prerequisite for Number of Islands (3) and Clone Graph (16). Teaches visited-set discipline and graph input parsing.

problems.title: Find Center of Star Graph
problems.note: O(1) solution: the center appears in every edge. Just check edges[0] and edges[1] for the common node. Teaches graph topology recognition — no traversal needed when structure is known.

problems.title: Binary Tree Preorder Traversal
problems.note: Visit root first, then left subtree, then right. Both recursive (trivial) and iterative (stack: push right then left). O(n). Prerequisite for Construct from Preorder/Inorder (67), Serialize Tree (52). The iterative form is required — always know it.

problems.title: Binary Tree Inorder Traversal
problems.note: Inorder of BST = sorted sequence. Iterative (explicit stack): push left chain, pop, visit, go right. O(n). Prerequisite for Validate BST (57), Kth Smallest in BST (54), Recover BST (70), Delete Node in BST (88). Iterative form must be automatic.

problems.title: Binary Tree Postorder Traversal
problems.note: Children before parent. Iterative: reverse of modified preorder (root, right, left) → reverse result. O(n). Prerequisite for Binary Tree Max Path Sum (53), Diameter (63), Binary Tree Cameras (75), House Robber III (79). Post-order = bottom-up computation.

problems.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal (BFS)
problems.note: Queue-based BFS. Snapshot queue.size() before inner loop to separate levels. O(n). Foundation for Right View (59), Zigzag (69), Vertical Order (58), Populating Next Pointers (68), Maximum Width (87). Must be completely automatic.

problems.title: Maximum Depth of Tree
problems.note: The canonical recursion problem. return 1 + max(left, right). Foundation for Balanced Binary Tree (71), Diameter (63), and all post-order DP problems. Must be solved both recursively and iteratively.

problems.title: Invert Binary Tree
problems.note: Swap left and right children at every node. Builds intuition for structural tree mutation. Prerequisite for Symmetric Tree (92) and tree comparison problems. Can also be solved iteratively with BFS.

problems.title: Balanced Binary Tree
problems.note: Naive O(n²) solution checks depth at every node. Optimal O(n) solution returns -1 as a sentinel on imbalance. Prerequisite for Convert Sorted List to BST (240). The sentinel trick is reused in many tree audit problems.

problems.title: Same Tree
problems.note: Check null equality first, then value, then recurse. Direct prerequisite for Subtree of Another Tree (93). Teaches the canonical 3-case recursive tree comparison pattern used in all structural equality problems.

problems.title: Two Sum (HashMap)
problems.note: Store each number and its index. For each element, check if complement exists in map. O(n) single pass. The canonical HashMap problem — teaches the 'store what you've seen, query complement' pattern. Prerequisite for Subarray Sum Equals K (133) and all hash-complement problems.

problems.title: Frequency Counter Pattern
problems.note: Count frequency of elements using HashMap or array[26]. Compare two frequency maps in O(1) by tracking a 'matches' counter. O(n). Direct prerequisite for Find All Anagrams (141), Group Anagrams (142), Top K Frequent Elements (104), and all sliding window frequency problems.

problems.title: Logger Rate Limiter
problems.note: HashMap of message → last_logged_time. O(1) lookup. Prerequisite for Design Hit Counter (103) and Distributed Rate Limiter (157). Key insight: don't need a queue — just track last timestamp per message.

problems.title: Insert Delete GetRandom
problems.note: Combine a HashMap (element → index) with an ArrayList for O(1) random access. Delete by swapping with the last element. Prerequisite for Random Pick Index (155). The swap-with-last trick is the key insight.

problems.title: Find All Anagrams
problems.note: Sliding window with frequency count comparison. Use a counter diff variable instead of comparing entire maps each step — O(n) total. Prerequisite for Minimum Window Substring (187) and all sliding window hashing problems.

problems.title: Group Anagrams
problems.note: Sort each word to get canonical key. O(n·k·log k) time. Alternative: use character frequency tuple as key for O(n·k). Prerequisite for Top K Frequent Words (149). Core pattern: canonical-form hashing.

problems.title: Implement Stack (Array-backed)
problems.note: push/pop/peek/isEmpty with a list. O(1) amortized. Foundation for Min Stack (145), Stack using Queues (143), Tree Inorder Iterative (84), Monotonic Stack problems. The call stack is also a stack — understanding this bridges recursion and iteration.

problems.title: Implement Queue (Array-backed)
problems.note: enqueue/dequeue/peek/isEmpty. Fixed-array: head/tail pointers mod capacity. Dynamic: use collections.deque. O(1). Foundation for Queue using Stacks (144), Circular Queue (140), BFS. Understanding deque enables O(1) both-end operations.

problems.title: Valid Parentheses
problems.note: Push on open bracket, match+pop on close. O(n). The canonical stack application problem. Prerequisite for Daily Temperatures, Asteroid Collision, and all monotonic stack problems. Teaches the 'stack stores unresolved state' mental model.

problems.title: Daily Temperatures (Monotonic Stack)
problems.note: Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. When a higher temperature is found, pop all indices with lower temperatures. O(n). Prerequisite for Largest Rectangle in Histogram (129), Trapping Rain Water (128), Sliding Window Max (110). The 'pending unresolved' stack pattern.

problems.title: Stack using Queues
problems.note: Push is O(n): enqueue then rotate all elements. Or use single queue with rotation on push. Prerequisite for understanding amortized complexity. Pair with Queue using Stacks (144) — both appear together in interviews.

problems.title: Queue using Stacks
problems.note: Two-stack approach: push stack and pop stack. Amortized O(1) pop via lazy transfer. Directly teaches amortized analysis. Prerequisite for Design Browser History (112). The lazy transfer is the key optimization to explain in interviews.

problems.title: Two Sum (Sorted Array)
problems.note: Left and right pointers moving inward. Sum too large → move right left; too small → move left right. O(n). Foundation for 3Sum, Container With Most Water, and Sort Colors (146). The sorted array is the key prerequisite enabling two-pointer movement decisions.

problems.title: Container With Most Water
problems.note: Move the shorter side inward — a taller bar can only help if the width doesn't shrink too much. O(n). Teaches the greedy two-pointer invariant: always move the constraining side. Prerequisite for Trapping Rain Water (128).

problems.title: Sliding Window — Fixed Size (Max Average Subarray)
problems.note: Maintain a running sum. Add incoming element, remove outgoing. O(n). Foundation for all fixed-window problems. Prerequisite for Max Points from Cards (264) and all variable-window problems. The add-incoming / subtract-outgoing template is universal.

problems.title: Sliding Window — Variable Size (Longest Substring No Repeat)
problems.note: Expand right pointer; shrink left when constraint violated. O(n). Foundation for Find All Anagrams (141), Longest Substring K (159), Min Window Substring (187), Repeating Char Replace (186). The expand-shrink loop structure is the template for all variable-window problems.

problems.title: Sort Colors
problems.note: Dutch National Flag algorithm. Three pointers: low, mid, high. Single pass O(n). Foundation for 3-way partition in QuickSort. Prerequisite for K Closest Points (147) sorting intuition and Kth Largest (116) partitioning.

problems.title: Fibonacci (Memoization → Tabulation)
problems.note: Naive recursion → memoization with dict → bottom-up tabulation → O(1) space with two vars. Walk through all 4 forms. Prerequisite for Climb Stairs (179), House Robber (178), Coin Change (163), and every 1D DP problem. The 4-step DP formulation must be internalized.

problems.title: Prefix Sum Array
problems.note: prefix[i] = sum of arr[0..i]. Range sum(l,r) = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. O(n) build, O(1) query. Prerequisite for Subarray Sum Equals K (133), Maximum Subarray (168), Range Sum 2D (135), and all prefix-based problems. The complement trick (prefix[r] - k → look up count) is critical.

problems.title: Binary Search (Standard + On Answer)
problems.note: Standard: lo=0, hi=n-1, mid=(lo+hi)//2. Decide which half to discard. O(log n). Binary search on answer: define feasibility function, search over answer space. Prerequisite for Search Rotated (188), Koko Bananas (195), Ship Packages (194), Median 2 Arrays (189). The lo≤hi vs lo<hi boundary choice matters.

problems.title: Buy/Sell Stock
problems.note: Track running minimum and update max profit. O(n) single pass. Foundation for all stock problems: with cooldown (258), with K transactions. The insight — profit = current − min_so_far — is the base case for all variants.

problems.title: Maximum Subarray
problems.note: Kadane's: reset to 0 when running sum goes negative. O(n). Prerequisite for Max Product Subarray (177) and Continuous Subarray Sum (134). The reset decision is the greedy insight that underpins all subarray optimization problems.

problems.title: Climb Stairs
problems.note: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Can be solved in O(1) space with two variables. The entry point for all 1D DP. Prerequisite for House Robber (178), Decode Ways (165), Coin Change (163). Teaches bottom-up DP construction.

problems.title: Reverse Nodes in k-Group
problems.note: Reverse exactly k nodes at a time, reconnect to next group. Check group size before reversing. Capstone for linked list pointer manipulation — requires mastery of Reverse Linked List (267), Find Middle (269).

problems.title: Reorder List
problems.note: Three phases: find middle, reverse second half, merge. Combines (269) and (267) in sequence. Databricks on-site problem. Must not use extra space — the O(n) space solution with an array is rejected.

problems.title: Sort List
problems.prerequisite: Merge Two Sorted Lists, Find Middle
problems.note: Bottom-up merge sort on linked lists avoids recursion stack. Top-down is O(log n) space; bottom-up is O(1). Requires (268) and (269) as building blocks. The bottom-up approach is what separates strong candidates.

problems.title: Double a Number Represented as a Linked List
problems.note: Reverse, double with carry propagation, reverse back. Alternatively use recursion to propagate carry from tail. Direct extension of Add Two Numbers (273). Tests carry handling at the head of the list.

problems.title: Odd Even Linked List
problems.note: Maintain two dummy heads for odd/even chains. Connect even tail to odd head at end. O(n) time, O(1) space. Uber screening problem. Tests pointer hygiene — must not lose the even head before connecting.

problems.title: Plus One Linked List
problems.note: Find rightmost non-9 node, increment it, set all subsequent nodes to 0. Sentinel dummy node handles the all-9s edge case. Simpler than reversing — teaches right-scan pattern for carry problems.

problems.title: Convert Sorted List to BST
problems.note: Use slow/fast pointer to find mid, recurse on left and right halves. O(n log n) naive; O(n) via in-order simulation with a pointer. Databricks values the O(n) approach. Requires (269) as prerequisite.

problems.title: Reverse Linked List
problems.note: Foundation for Reorder List (235), Sort List (236), Reverse Nodes in k-Group (234). Must be mastered before any other LL problem.

problems.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
problems.note: Direct prerequisite for Sort List (236) and Merge K Sorted Lists (111). Teaches pointer rewiring without losing references.

problems.title: Find Middle of Linked List
problems.note: Fast/slow pointer technique. Prerequisite for Sort List (236), Reorder List (235), Convert Sorted List to BST (240), and Palindrome Linked List (272).

problems.title: Linked List Cycle Detection
problems.note: Floyd's two-pointer cycle detection. Also covers finding the cycle entry node. Asked in every on-site across all target companies.

problems.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
problems.note: One-pass solution using two pointers separated by n steps. Tests off-by-one discipline and dummy node usage.

problems.title: Palindrome Linked List
problems.note: Combines three fundamentals in sequence. Capstone problem for Level 1 LL: find mid, reverse second half, compare, restore (optional).

problems.title: Add Two Numbers
problems.note: Digit-by-digit carry propagation. Directly prerequisite for Double a Number Represented as a Linked List (237). Asked at Razorpay financial rounds.

problems.title: Copy List with Random Pointer
problems.pattern: Deep Copy with Hash Map or Interleaving
problems.note: Two approaches: O(n) space with hashmap, or O(1) space with node interleaving. The O(1) approach is the Palantir bar signal.

problems.title: Find First True in Boolean Array (Binary Search Template)
problems.note: Binary search where answers form FFFFTTTT pattern. lo=left boundary, hi=right boundary, find first T. O(log n). The universal template for all 'binary search on answer' problems. Prerequisite for Ship Packages (194), Koko (195), Split Array (196). Separate: find first True vs find last False.

problems.title: Bit Manipulation Basics (AND, OR, XOR, Shifts)
problems.note: Know: a&b (common bits), a|b (union), a^b (difference), a<<k (*2^k), a>>k (//2^k), ~a, a&1 (check odd), a&(a-1) (clear lowest set bit), a&(-a) (isolate lowest set bit). Prerequisite for Single Number (253), Missing Number (254), Number of 1 Bits (255), Maximum XOR (280).

problems.title: Union-Find Basics (without optimization)
problems.note: parent[] array. find(): walk up until parent[x]==x. union(a,b): set parent[find(a)] = find(b). O(n) worst case find without optimization. Must understand this naively before adding rank + path compression (249). The slow version teaches the concept; the fast version teaches optimization.

problems.title: Number of Islands
problems.representation_note: IMPLICIT GRAPH — do NOT build an explicit adjacency list. The grid IS the graph. Each cell is a node; edges are implicit 4-directional neighbors.
problems.note: The foundational grid DFS/BFS problem. In-place marking (set '1' to '0') avoids a visited array. Prerequisite for Rotting Oranges (11), Pacific Atlantic (17), and all multi-source BFS grid problems. Master both DFS and Union-Find approaches.

problems.title: Rotting Oranges
problems.note: Seed BFS queue with all initially rotten cells. Count fresh oranges; decrement on each spread. If fresh > 0 at end, return -1. Prerequisite for Shortest Bridge (29) and Shortest Path in Grid (12). DoorDash delivery spread simulation.

problems.title: Shortest Path in 2D Grid
problems.note: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted grids. Mark visited at enqueue time, not dequeue. Prerequisite for Shortest Path with Obstacles (12) and Path With Minimum Effort (20). Foundation of all grid BFS problems.

problems.title: All Nodes Distance K
problems.note: Convert tree to undirected graph by building parent pointers, then BFS from target. Key insight: trees are graphs — add reverse edges. Prerequisite for All Nodes Distance K in Binary Tree (244) and Infection Time (80).

problems.title: Is Graph Bipartite?
problems.representation_note: ADJACENCY LIST is correct here. Bipartite check only needs neighbor iteration.
problems.representation_decision: Sparse unknown-density graph → Adjacency list default
problems.note: 2-color BFS/DFS. If any edge connects same-colored nodes, not bipartite. Prerequisite for Possible Bipartition (25). Must handle disconnected graphs — run BFS/DFS from every unvisited node.

problems.title: Clone Graph
problems.representation_note: ADJACENCY LIST is the natural choice — input is already given as adjacency list.
problems.representation_decision: Unknown density → Adjacency list (input format drives choice)
problems.note: HashMap from original node to its clone prevents revisiting. BFS layer by layer. Prerequisite for Copy List with Random Pointer (274) conceptually. Palantir tests the O(1)-space interleaving approach as a follow-up.

problems.title: Snake and Ladder
problems.note: Flatten 2D board to 1D array with Boustrophedon numbering. BFS for minimum moves — don't revisit squares. Prerequisite for Open the Lock (26) and Minimum Genetic Mutation (39). The board-flattening is the hardest implementation step.

problems.title: Open the Lock
problems.note: Each lock state is a BFS node; each dial turn is an edge. Use a HashSet for deadends and visited states. Bidirectional BFS reduces search space from O(8^4) to O(2·8^2). Prerequisite for Word Ladder (7).

problems.title: Graph Valid Tree
problems.note: A valid tree has exactly n-1 edges and is connected. Use Union-Find: if union of any edge finds they're already connected, there's a cycle. Prerequisite for Redundant Connection (18) and Redundant Connection II (214).

problems.title: All Paths From Source
problems.note: Backtracking DFS on a DAG. Add path to result when target is reached. Prerequisite for Word Ladder II (43) and all path enumeration problems. Key: DAG guarantees no cycles so no visited set needed.

problems.title: Construct Quad Tree
problems.note: Check if all cells in region are uniform. If yes, leaf node. Else, recurse into 4 quadrants. Prerequisite for Quad Tree Implementation (220). Uber uses this for spatial indexing problems. 2D prefix sums make uniformity check O(1).

problems.title: Serialize/Deserialize Tree
problems.note: Pre-order DFS with null markers, or BFS level-order. Deserialize by rebuilding from the same traversal order. Prerequisite for Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree (253). Databricks cares about the BFS variant for wide trees.

problems.title: Lowest Common Ancestor
problems.note: If root is p or q, root is LCA. If p found in left and q in right subtree, root is LCA. Foundation for LCA of N-ary Tree (76), LCA IV (94), and BST LCA (251). Prerequisite for all ancestry path problems.

problems.title: BST Insert and Search
problems.note: Search: go left if target < node, right if greater. Insert: same logic, create node when null. O(h) = O(log n) balanced, O(n) worst case. Prerequisite for Validate BST (57), Delete Node (88), Kth Smallest (54), Convert List to BST (82). BST invariant: left < node < right for ALL ancestors.

problems.title: BST Inorder = Sorted Array
problems.note: Inorder traversal of any valid BST yields elements in sorted ascending order. This property is the foundation of Validate BST (57), Kth Smallest (54), Recover BST (70), Minimum Distance (95), Range Sum of BST (100), BST Iterator (219). Internalize it as an axiom.

problems.title: Validate BST
problems.note: Pass min/max bounds through recursion — not just parent comparison. Common bug: checking only against parent allows invalid BSTs to pass. Prerequisite for Recover BST (70) and all BST validation problems.

problems.title: Smallest K Elements BST
problems.note: In-order traversal yields sorted order; stop at K-th element. With augmented BST storing subtree sizes: O(log n + k). Prerequisite for Kth Smallest Element in BST (54) and BST Iterator (219).

problems.title: Binary Tree Vertical Order
problems.note: BFS with column index (root=0, left=-1, right=+1). Store in TreeMap for sorted column order. Prerequisite for Vertical Order Traversal (249). Databricks asks both variants — know the difference (sorting within column).

problems.title: Binary Tree Right View
problems.note: BFS: take the last element at each level. DFS: visit right child first, record first node at each depth. Prerequisite for Binary Tree Zigzag (69). Both approaches must be known — BFS for clarity, DFS for follow-up.

problems.title: Tree Level Order Traversal
problems.note: BFS with level size tracking (snapshot queue.size() before inner loop). Foundation for Right View (59), Zigzag (69), Maximum Width (87), Populating Next Pointers (68). Must be automatic.

problems.title: Tree Inorder Iterative
problems.note: Push nodes onto stack while going left; pop and visit, then go right. Foundation for Smallest K in BST (148), BST Iterator (219), Recover BST (70). Must be solved without recursion — this is the explicit stack simulation of the call stack.

problems.title: Flatten Tree to List
problems.note: Morris traversal (O(1) space): find pre-order predecessor, thread right pointer. Or post-order: flatten right, flatten left, attach right to left's tail. Prerequisite for all tree-to-list conversion problems.

problems.title: Populating Next Pointers
problems.note: O(1) space: use already-populated next pointers of parent level to traverse without a queue. Key insight: process node's children using parent's next chain. Prerequisite for all level-linking tree problems.

problems.title: Path Sum II
problems.note: Backtracking DFS: add node to path, recurse, remove on return. Check at leaf nodes. Prerequisite for Path Sum III (99) and K-Sum Paths (73). The backtrack-on-return pattern is reused in all path enumeration problems.

problems.title: Sum Root to Leaf
problems.note: Accumulate number as you descend (current = prev*10 + node.val). Add to total at leaf. Prerequisite for Pseudo-Palindromic Paths (89) and all path-value accumulation problems.

problems.title: Binary Tree Paths
problems.note: DFS with string path accumulation. Add '->' separator at each step, record at leaf. Prerequisite for Smallest String From Leaf (85) and Binary Tree Max Path Sum (53). Teaches path-string construction pattern.

problems.title: Convert List to BST
problems.note: O(n log n) approach: find middle, recurse. O(n) approach: in-order simulate with a pointer advancing through the sorted list. The O(n) approach requires understanding in-order traversal order matches sorted list order.

problems.title: Delete Node in BST
problems.note: Three cases: leaf (remove), one child (replace), two children (replace with in-order successor/predecessor). Prerequisite for Range Sum of BST (100) pruning logic. The two-children case is the tricky one interviewers focus on.

problems.title: Subtree of Another Tree
problems.note: For each node in the main tree, check if it matches the subtree root with Same Tree logic. O(m·n). Optimal: serialize both trees and use string matching. Prerequisite for Leaf-Similar Trees (98).

problems.title: Doubly Linked List (insert, delete, move-to-front)
problems.note: Each node has prev and next pointers. Remove: prev.next=node.next, next.prev=node.prev. O(1). Foundation for LRU Cache (101) and LFU Cache (102). The key insight: doubly linked list enables O(1) removal of any node when you already have a pointer to it — unlike singly linked list.

problems.title: LRU Cache
problems.note: Doubly linked list (for O(1) removal) + HashMap (key → node). On get/put, move node to head. On capacity exceeded, remove tail node. Prerequisite for LFU Cache (102). The dummy head/tail sentinels prevent null checks.

problems.title: Design Hit Counter
problems.note: Queue of timestamps, remove those older than 300 seconds. Or circular buffer of 300 slots (timestamp % 300). Prerequisite for Distributed Rate Limiter (157). The circular buffer approach is O(1) time and O(300) space — preferred.

problems.title: Underground System
problems.note: Two hashmaps: (id → (station, checkInTime)) and (route → (totalTime, count)). Average time = totalTime/count. Uber on-site problem for transit systems. Tests multi-key hashmap design and running average computation.

problems.title: Min-Heap / Max-Heap Operations
problems.note: Heap property: parent ≤ children (min-heap). insert: append + sift-up. extractMin: swap root with last, sift-down. O(log n) both. In Python: heapq (min-heap); negate for max-heap. Prerequisite for Top K Frequent (104), Task Scheduler (115), Kth Largest (116), K Closest (147), Merge K Lists (111).

problems.title: Kth Largest Element (Heap Template)
problems.note: Maintain a min-heap of size K. If incoming > heap top, pop and push. Final heap top = Kth largest. O(n log k). The template for all 'top-K' problems. Prerequisite for Top K Frequent Elements (104), K Closest Points (147), and Smallest Range (197). Know both heap and QuickSelect approaches.

problems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.note: Frequency HashMap + min-heap of size K. Or bucket sort: frequency → list of elements, O(n). Prerequisite for Top K Frequent Words (149) and Merge K Sorted Lists (111). Bucket sort variant is O(n) and often expected as follow-up.

problems.title: Task Scheduler
problems.note: Max-heap of frequencies. Each cycle: pop up to n+1 tasks, decrement, re-push if > 0. Add idle slots if fewer than n+1 tasks in heap. Prerequisite for Task Scheduler II (190) and Single-Threaded CPU (138). The cooldown simulation is the core pattern.

problems.title: Kth Largest in Array
problems.note: Min-heap of size K in O(n log k). QuickSelect in O(n) average. Prerequisite for K Closest Points (147) and Smallest Range (197). Know both — heap for online streams, QuickSelect for one-shot queries. QuickSelect pivot choice matters.

problems.title: K Closest Points
problems.note: Max-heap of size K by distance. Eject when size > K. Or QuickSelect for O(n) average. Uber geospatial screening question. Prerequisite for Smallest Range Covering K Lists (197). Compare by squared distance to avoid sqrt overhead.

problems.title: Design Browser History
problems.note: Two stacks (back, forward) or a list with a current index. On visit, clear forward stack. Prerequisite for Text Editor Logic (153) and all undo/redo design problems. The list+index approach is cleaner for follow-ups with limits.

problems.title: Min Stack
problems.note: Two stacks: main and min-tracker. Push to min stack only when new value ≤ current min. Or single stack of (value, current_min) pairs. Prerequisite for Sliding Window Max (110) conceptually. The paired-stack approach is O(n) space in worst case.

problems.title: Random Pick (Weight)
problems.note: Build prefix sum array; binary search for the target bucket using random float in [0, total). Prerequisite for Random Pick Index (155). The key insight: weight ranges in prefix sum array correspond directly to probability ranges.

problems.title: Random Pick Index
problems.note: Reservoir sampling: when nth occurrence found, pick it with probability 1/n. O(1) space, O(n) time per pick. Prerequisite for Random Pick with Weight (120) conceptually. The reservoir trick is used when the input stream is unknown length.

problems.title: Running Sum / Prefix Sum Basics
problems.note: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]. Range query in O(1). HashMap storing prefix_sum → first_index enables O(n) subarray sum problems. Prerequisite for Subarray Sum Equals K (133), Continuous Subarray Sum (134), Range Sum 2D (135), Product Except Self (132). The HashMap variant is the most powerful form.

problems.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
problems.note: HashMap of prefix sum → count. For each index, check if (prefix_sum - k) exists in map. O(n). Prerequisite for Continuous Subarray Sum (134) and Range Sum Query 2D (135). The complement lookup is the canonical prefix sum pattern.

problems.title: Circular Deque
problems.note: Array with head/tail pointers. Size check on insert. head = (head - 1 + capacity) % capacity for insertFront. Prerequisite for Sliding Window Maximum (110) which uses a deque internally. Teaches modular arithmetic for circular buffers.

problems.title: Circular Queue
problems.note: Fixed-size array with head, tail, and size/count. Enqueue at tail % capacity, dequeue at head. Prerequisite for Circular Deque (139). Foundation for understanding ring buffers used in OS scheduling.

problems.title: Majority Element II
problems.note: Extended Boyer-Moore: maintain two candidates and two counts. After pass, verify both candidates actually appear > n/3 times. At most 2 elements can appear > n/3 times. Prerequisite for any generalized majority voting problem.

problems.title: Jump Game
problems.note: Track max reachable index. If current index > max reachable, return false. O(n). Prerequisite for Jump Game II (min jumps). Teaches greedy reachability — the invariant is always: can we reach or surpass the current index?

problems.title: Sort Intervals by Start Time
problems.note: Sort intervals by start time. Enables linear scan to find overlaps. O(n log n). Foundation for Merge Intervals (171), Insert Interval (172), Meeting Rooms II (170), Job Scheduling (192). Without this sort, detecting overlaps requires O(n²). Always sort first.

problems.title: Meeting Rooms II
problems.note: Sort by start time. Min-heap of end times. At each meeting, if earliest-ending room ends before current start, reuse it. Heap size = rooms needed. Prerequisite for Task Scheduler (115) resource analogy. Also solvable with event-timeline sweep.

problems.title: Merge Intervals
problems.note: Sort by start. Merge if current start ≤ last end. Update last end to max. O(n log n). Prerequisite for Insert Interval (172), Meeting Rooms II (170), and Range Module (193). The sort-then-sweep pattern is universal for interval problems.

problems.title: Insert Interval
problems.note: Three phases: add all intervals that end before new start, merge all that overlap, add rest. O(n). Requires (171) conceptually. Tests boundary conditions: off-by-one errors in overlap detection are common failure mode.

problems.title: Anagram Check (Two Strings)
problems.note: Count frequency of each character in both strings. Compare maps. O(n). Or sort both and compare. Prerequisite for Find All Anagrams (141) and Group Anagrams (142). The frequency comparison is O(26) = O(1) — faster than sorting. The sorted-canonical-key variant generalizes to groups.

problems.title: Longest Palindrome
problems.note: Count characters with odd frequency. Length = total - (odd_count - 1). O(n). Prerequisite for Longest Palindromic Substring (Manacher's). Teaches parity-based character counting — all even-count chars can be used, plus one odd-count center.

problems.title: Search Rotated Array
problems.note: Determine which half is sorted, then check if target is in that half. O(log n). Prerequisite for Find Minimum in Rotated Array. Key insight: one half is always sorted — use that to make the binary decision.

problems.title: Ship Package Capacity
problems.note: Binary search on capacity in [max(weights), sum(weights)]. Greedy feasibility check: simulate shipping with given capacity. Prerequisite for Split Array Max Sum (196) and Koko Eating Bananas (195). The feasibility function is the key insight.

problems.title: Koko Eating Bananas
problems.note: Binary search on speed k in [1, max(piles)]. Feasibility: sum(ceil(pile/k)) ≤ h. O(n log m). Pair with Ship Packages (194) — same binary search on answer pattern with different feasibility check.

problems.title: Implement Union-Find (Rank + Path Compression)
problems.note: Path compression (find) + union by rank. Near O(1) amortized per operation. Foundation for Number of Islands II (4), Accounts Merge (24), Redundant Connection (18), and all DSU problems. Must implement from scratch — not using a library.

problems.title: Single Number
problems.note: XOR all elements. Pairs cancel (a^a=0), single remains. O(n) time, O(1) space. Prerequisite for Missing Number (254) and all XOR-based tricks. The identity a^0=a and a^a=0 are the only two facts needed.

problems.title: Missing Number
problems.note: XOR all indices 0..n with all values. The missing number remains. Or use Gauss formula: n*(n+1)/2 - sum. Both O(n) time, O(1) space. Prerequisite for Find Duplicate Number (131). XOR approach generalizes better.

problems.title: Number of 1 Bits
problems.note: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. Count how many times until n becomes 0. O(number of set bits). Prerequisite for Maximum XOR (280). The n&(n-1) trick also checks if n is a power of 2 (result = 0).

problems.title: Next Greater Element I
problems.note: For each element, find the first greater element to its right. Monotonic decreasing stack: pop when a greater element is found. O(n). Builds directly on Daily Temperatures. Prerequisite for Largest Rectangle Histogram (129) and Trapping Rain Water (128). Master the stack-pop-on-resolve loop.

problems.title: Bus Routes
problems.note: Model routes as nodes, not stops. BFS on route graph: if two routes share a stop, they're adjacent. O(sum of route lengths). Prerequisite for Multi-Source BFS (207). The key insight: changing levels of abstraction (stop → route) reduces edge count.

problems.title: Evaluate Division
problems.note: Build weighted undirected graph: a/b = k → edge a→b with weight k, edge b→a with weight 1/k. BFS/DFS to find path product. O(V+E) per query. Databricks conversion chains. Prerequisite for Maximize Amount After Conversions (227) with Bellman-Ford.

problems.title: Topological Sort — Kahn's BFS
problems.prerequisite: BFS on Graph (G02), Detect Cycle in Directed Graph (G05)
problems.note: Build in-degree array. Enqueue all 0-in-degree nodes. Process: dequeue, add to result, decrement neighbors' in-degrees, enqueue new 0-in-degree. If result size < V, cycle exists. O(V+E). Prerequisite for Course Schedule II (6), Alien Dictionary (2), Parallel Courses III (36). Must be automatic.

problems.title: Topological Sort — DFS Post-order
problems.note: DFS with 3-color states. Push to result stack on finish (post-order). Reverse gives topological order. O(V+E). Equivalent to Kahn's but DFS-based — preferred for cycle detection with explicit back-edge identification. Prerequisite for Alien Dictionary (2) and Find Eventual Safe States (22).

problems.title: Alien Dictionary
problems.note: Build directed graph from adjacent word pairs. Compare char by char — first difference gives an edge. Detect cycles (invalid input). Kahn's BFS topo sort. Prerequisite for Course Schedule II (6) and Sequence Reconstruction (37). Edge case: prefix word before shorter word is invalid.

problems.title: Course Schedule II
problems.note: Kahn's BFS: in-degree array, enqueue all 0-in-degree nodes. Output order = topological order. If not all nodes processed, cycle exists. Prerequisite for Parallel Courses III (36), Largest Color Value (35), and Build Rooms (191).

problems.title: Largest Color Value
problems.note: Topological order + DP. dp[node][color] = max count of that color on any path to this node. Propagate through topo order. O(V·26 + E). If cycle, return -1. Combines Kahn's topo sort with color-frequency DP.

problems.title: Parallel Courses III
problems.note: Topo sort + DP: dp[node] = time[node] + max(dp[prerequisites]). Answer = max(dp[all nodes]). O(V+E). Prerequisite for Build Rooms Ways (191). The critical path in a DAG — project management interview framing.

problems.title: Sequence Reconstruction
problems.note: Check if the target sequence is the UNIQUE topological sort. At each step in Kahn's BFS, if ever more than 1 node has in-degree 0, the sort is not unique. O(V+E). The uniqueness check (queue size ≤ 1) is the key modification.

problems.title: Build Rooms Ways
problems.note: For each node, count orderings = (subtree_size - 1)! / product(child_subtree_sizes). Multinomial coefficient + topo sort. O(n). Uses modular inverse for division. The combinatorics insight: relative order within subtrees is independent.

problems.title: Number of Islands II
problems.note: Online version: add land cells one by one, union with 4-directional neighbors. Track island count: +1 on add, -1 per successful union. O(α(n)) per operation. Prerequisite for Accounts Merge (24) and Smallest String with Swaps (213).

problems.title: Redundant Connection
problems.note: Process edges in order. First edge whose endpoints are already connected forms the cycle — that's the redundant connection. Union-Find with path compression. Prerequisite for Redundant Connection II (214) which handles directed graphs.

problems.title: Accounts Merge
problems.note: Union all emails in an account under the first email. HashMap: email → canonical root. Group emails by root. Sort each group. O(n·α(n) + n log n). Databricks data deduplication problem. Prerequisite for Similar String Groups (243).

problems.title: Count Unreachable Pairs
problems.note: Find all connected components and their sizes. For each component of size s, pairs with other components = s * (total - s). Sum and divide by 2. O(V+E). Or Union-Find. Teaches component-size counting for pair-counting problems.

problems.title: Word Ladder
problems.representation_note: IMPLICIT GRAPH — generate neighbors on-the-fly during BFS by mutating each character position.
problems.representation_decision: Implicit string graph → On-the-fly neighbor generation (never build adjacency structure)
problems.note: BFS on word states. For each word, try replacing each character with 'a'-'z'. O(n·L²) where L = word length. Prerequisite for Word Ladder II (43) and Minimum Genetic Mutation (39). Bidirectional BFS halves the branching factor.

problems.title: Minimum Genetic Mutation
problems.note: Same pattern as Word Ladder (7) but with 4-character genes and only 4 possible mutations (A/C/G/T). BFS from start to end, only through valid bank genes. O(bank_size × gene_length × 4). Directly analogous to Word Ladder.

problems.title: Word Ladder II
problems.note: BFS to build layer graph (parent map), then backtrack from end to start to collect all shortest paths. O(n × L² + paths). Key: only traverse edges that stay within BFS levels — prevents non-shortest paths. Harder than Word Ladder (7).

problems.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
problems.note: Min-heap of (distance, node). Relax neighbors: if dist[u]+w < dist[v], update and enqueue. Skip stale entries (dist > known). O((V+E) log V). Prerequisite for Network Delay Time (8), Path With Minimum Effort (20), Path With Max Probability (38), Cheapest Flights (215). Only works with non-negative weights.

problems.title: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
problems.note: Relax all edges V-1 times. Handles negative weights. V-th relaxation detects negative cycle. O(V·E). Prerequisite for Cheapest Flights K Stops (215) (K+1 iterations), Maximize Amount After Conversions (227). Slower than Dijkstra but more general — know when each applies.

problems.title: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
problems.note: dp[i][j] = min cost from i to j. Try every intermediate node k: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]). O(V³). Prerequisite for Smallest Neighbors Threshold (30). Use when V ≤ 400 and you need all-pairs shortest paths. The k-loop must be outermost.

problems.title: Network Delay Time
problems.note: Dijkstra with min-heap. Single source, all destinations. Answer = max of all shortest paths. If any node unreachable, return -1. Prerequisite for Path With Max Probability (38) and Min Cost to Destination (42). Foundation for all weighted shortest path problems.

problems.title: Path With Minimum Effort
problems.note: Modified Dijkstra: relax by max(current_effort, edge_weight) instead of sum. Or binary search on effort + BFS feasibility. O(m·n·log(m·n)). Prerequisite for Path With Max Probability (38). The objective function change in Dijkstra is the key insight.

problems.title: Smallest Neighbors (Threshold)
problems.note: Floyd-Warshall for all-pairs shortest paths, then count neighbors within threshold per city. City with fewest neighbors = answer. O(n³). Or Dijkstra from each node. Floyd-Warshall preferred when n ≤ 100 and edge weights are sparse.

problems.title: Path With Max Probability
problems.note: Modified Dijkstra with max-heap: relax by multiplication (prob[u] * edge_weight) instead of addition. O((V+E) log V). Prerequisite for Maximize Amount After Conversions (227). The objective change (sum→product) in Dijkstra is the key insight.

problems.title: Number of Restrictive Paths
problems.note: Dijkstra from destination to get dist[]. Then DFS/DP: count paths where each step goes to a node with strictly smaller dist[]. Memoize. O((V+E) log V + V + E). Combines Dijkstra preprocessing with DAG path counting.

problems.title: Min Cost to Destination
problems.note: Dijkstra with state (node, zones_crossed). Different cost tiers based on zones. Or SPFA for dynamic cost graphs. Prerequisite for Cheapest Flights Within K Stops (215). The state augmentation pattern for cost-tier problems.

problems.title: Critical Connections
problems.note: Tarjan's bridge-finding: track discovery time and low value. Edge (u,v) is a bridge if low[v] > disc[u]. O(V+E). Prerequisite for Critical Connections in a Network (206). Palantir infrastructure reliability question. Must know DFS timestamps.

problems.title: Detonate Maximum Bombs
problems.note: Build directed graph: edge i→j if bomb i's radius reaches bomb j's center. BFS/DFS from each bomb, count reachable bombs. Return max count. O(n³) precompute + O(n²) BFS. Prerequisite for Detonate Maximum Bombs directed reachability (245).

problems.title: Minimum Degree of Connect
problems.note: Check connectivity after removing each single node, then each pair of nodes. O(V × (V+E)) for single-node removal. If graph remains connected with no removals, answer is 3. Prerequisite for Critical Connections (10) pattern.

problems.title: Shortest Path in a Grid
problems.representation_note: IMPLICIT GRAPH — BFS/Dijkstra directly on grid coordinates.
problems.representation_decision: Grid → Direct coordinate BFS/Dijkstra (no adjacency structure)
problems.note: BFS with state (row, col, obstacles_remaining). State space = O(m·n·k). Prerequisite for Shortest Path with Restricted Access (208). The state augmentation pattern (add constraint to BFS state) generalizes to all constrained grid paths.

problems.title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
problems.note: Reverse: flow UP from ocean borders. BFS/DFS from all Pacific edges, then all Atlantic edges. Intersection = answer. O(m·n). Prerequisite for Shortest Bridge (29). The reversal insight (instead of checking every cell) is the key optimization.

problems.title: Minimum Knight Moves
problems.note: BFS from (0,0) to (x,y). Mirror to first quadrant by symmetry — reduces search space by 4x. Add buffer (-1,-1) to (-2,-2) to avoid boundary issues. O(x·y). Bidirectional BFS from both source and target halves depth.

problems.title: Shortest Bridge
problems.note: DFS to find first island, add all its cells to BFS queue. Then multi-source BFS to expand until second island hit. O(m·n). Combines DFS island finding + BFS shortest path. Prerequisite for shortest path between components problems.

problems.title: Minimum Height Trees
problems.note: Topological peeling from leaves inward. At most 2 roots remain (tree centroid). O(n). Similar to finding tree diameter — the root(s) of MHT are always the center(s) of the longest path. Prerequisite for all tree-center problems.

problems.title: Kruskal's MST Algorithm
problems.note: Sort all edges by weight. Add edge if endpoints in different components (union-find). Stop when V-1 edges added. O(E log E). Prerequisite for Min Cost to Connect Points (19) and Redundant Connection (18). The greedy correctness: adding cheapest safe edge always works (cut property).

problems.title: Prim's MST Algorithm
problems.note: Min-heap of (weight, node). Start from any node. At each step, add cheapest edge to unvisited node. O((V+E) log V). Preferred over Kruskal's for dense graphs (avoids sorting E² edges). Prerequisite for Min Cost to Connect Points (19). Very similar structure to Dijkstra's.

problems.title: Min Cost to Connect Points
problems.representation_note: EDGE LIST required for Kruskal's. Use Prim's with adjacency matrix or lazy heap for dense graphs.
problems.representation_decision: Dense complete graph → Edge list for Kruskal's; Adjacency list + min-heap for Prim's
problems.note: Complete graph with Manhattan distances. Prim's with lazy heap: O(n² log n). Kruskal's: O(n² log n) due to sorting n² edges. Prerequisite for all MST problems. The dense graph variant — Prim's avoids materializing all edges.

problems.title: Find Eventual Safe States
problems.note: A node is safe if it's not in a cycle. Reverse graph + topological sort via Kahn's (nodes with no out-edges in reverse = safe). Or DFS with 3 colors (white/gray/black). Prerequisite for Alien Dictionary (2) cycle detection.

problems.title: Reorder Routes
problems.note: Build undirected graph, mark original direction. BFS from node 0. If traversing an edge in its original direction, count it (needs reversal). O(n). Teaches directed-vs-undirected dual representation pattern.

problems.title: Longest Increasing Path
problems.note: DFS with memoization on each cell. No visited set needed — strictly increasing values prevent cycles. O(m·n). Prerequisite for Largest Color Value (35) and Longest Path in Matrix (50). The DAG property (strict increase) is what makes memoization safe.

problems.title: Longest Path in Matrix
problems.note: Sort cells by value. Process in increasing order. For each cell, extend from neighbors with smaller value. DP with topological ordering by value. O(m·n·log(m·n)). Related to Longest Increasing Path (32) — both exploit DAG structure from ordering.

problems.title: Possible Bipartition
problems.note: Model dislikes as undirected edges. BFS/DFS 2-coloring. If same color on both ends of an edge, impossible. Or DSU: union all people disliked by a person, check no person is in same component as disliked. Builds on Is Graph Bipartite? (14).

problems.title: Flower Planting No Adjacent
problems.note: Each node has degree ≤ 3 and 4 colors available. Greedy: for each garden, pick any color not used by neighbors. Always possible (pigeonhole: max 3 neighbors, 4 colors). O(V+E). The degree constraint guarantees greedy works.

problems.title: Trie Node Structure and Insert/Search
problems.note: TrieNode: children=dict or array[26], is_end=False. Insert: traverse/create nodes char by char. Search: traverse, return is_end. startsWith: traverse, return True if path exists. O(L) per operation. Prerequisite for Implement Trie (106), Map Sum Pairs (49), Autocomplete (108), Maximum XOR (280). Must be implemented from scratch.

problems.title: Map Sum Pairs
problems.note: Trie where each node stores the sum of values of all keys with that prefix. On insert, update all nodes along the path. O(L) insert and query. Prerequisite for Search Autocomplete (108) and Design Search Autocomplete System (216).

problems.title: Implement Trie
problems.note: TrieNode with children[26] and is_end flag. Insert, search, startsWith all O(L). Foundation for Design Add and Search Words (281), Search Autocomplete (108), Map Sum Pairs (49), Maximum XOR (280). Must implement from scratch.

problems.title: Search Autocomplete
problems.note: Trie where each node stores top-3 sentences by frequency. On insert, update top-3 at every node along path. Query: return top-3 at prefix node. O(L) query after O(n·L) build. Prerequisite for Design Search Autocomplete System (216). The precomputed top-K per node is the key optimization.

problems.title: Design File System
problems.note: HashMap from path → value, or Trie of path components. On createPath, verify parent exists. O(L) per operation with hashmap. The parent-existence check is the key constraint — split path and verify prefix exists.

problems.title: Top K Frequent Words
problems.note: Frequency HashMap + min-heap of size K with custom comparator (freq descending, then word ascending). O(n log k). Or bucket sort + trie for sorted order. Prerequisite for Search Autocomplete (108). The lexicographic tiebreaker in the comparator is the tricky part.

problems.title: Maximum XOR of Two Numbers — XOR Trie
problems.note: Build trie of binary representations. For each number, greedily try to take the opposite bit at each trie node. O(n·32). The greedy at each bit level works because higher bits dominate XOR value. Builds on Implement Trie (106) and bit manipulation (255).

problems.title: Design Add and Search Words — Wildcard Trie
problems.note: Trie with DFS for '.' wildcard — try all 26 children. O(26^L) worst case with many wildcards, O(L) for exact match. Builds on Implement Trie (106). The DFS branching on '.' is the key complexity driver — prune with early returns.

problems.title: Binary Tree Max Path Sum
problems.note: Post-order: for each node, compute max gain from left and right (clamp at 0). Update global max with node + left_gain + right_gain. Return node + max(left, right) gain upward. O(n). Key: the local optimum (upward path) differs from global (through node).

problems.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
problems.note: Post-order: diameter through a node = left_height + right_height. Update global max. Return height = 1 + max(left, right). O(n). Key: diameter does NOT always pass through root — must track global max separately from the return value.

problems.title: K-Sum Paths
problems.note: DFS with running prefix sum. HashMap: prefix_sum → count. At each node, check if (current_sum - k) exists in map. Add current_sum to map, recurse, remove on backtrack. O(n). Extends Subarray Sum Equals K (133) from arrays to trees.

problems.title: Subtree with Deepest Nodes
problems.note: Post-order: return (depth, LCA_of_deepest). If both subtrees have same max depth, current node is LCA. Else return the deeper side's LCA. O(n). Combines depth calculation with LCA finding in a single post-order pass.

problems.title: Smallest String From Leaf
problems.note: DFS collecting path. At leaf, build string (reverse path = leaf-to-root). Compare with global minimum. O(n·h) where h = height. Key: the string is built leaf-to-root (reversed), so comparing must account for direction.

problems.title: Pseudo-Palindromic Paths
problems.note: Use a bitmask to track parity of digit frequencies. XOR each digit in/out. At leaf, path is pseudo-palindromic if bitmask has at most one set bit (bitmask & (bitmask-1) == 0). O(n). Bitmask replaces frequency array — 10 digits = 10 bits.

problems.title: Path Sum III
problems.note: Same as K-Sum Paths (73). DFS with running prefix sum and HashMap. At each node check complement. Backtrack by removing prefix sum on return. O(n). The path need not start at root or end at leaf — the prefix sum approach handles this generalization.

problems.title: Kth Smallest Element in BST
problems.note: In-order traversal gives sorted order. Return Kth element. Iterative with explicit stack for O(1) extra space (aside from stack). With augmented BST (subtree size): O(log n). Prerequisite for BST Iterator (219) and Count of Smaller Numbers (60).

problems.title: Maximum Subtree BST
problems.note: Post-order: for each node, return (is_bst, min_val, max_val, size). A node forms a valid BST if both subtrees are valid BSTs and node.val satisfies min/max constraints. O(n). The 4-tuple return is the standard BST audit pattern.

problems.title: Count Nodes in Complete Tree
problems.note: For complete binary tree: compare leftmost height vs rightmost height. If equal, subtree is perfect: count = 2^h - 1. Else recurse on both subtrees. O(log² n). The perfect-subtree shortcut is what separates the O(log² n) solution from naive O(n).

problems.title: Minimum Distance in BST
problems.note: In-order traversal gives sorted sequence. Minimum distance = min gap between consecutive elements. Track previous node in traversal. O(n). Or for min distance to a target value: BST binary search narrowing. Both patterns must be known.

problems.title: Range Sum of BST
problems.note: DFS with BST pruning: skip left subtree if node.val ≤ low, skip right if node.val ≥ high. O(n) worst case but much faster in practice. The BST-property-based pruning is what makes this O(k + log n) where k = nodes in range.

problems.title: Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree)
problems.note: 1-indexed array. update(i, delta): i += i&(-i). query(i): sum 0..i by i -= i&(-i). O(log n) both. Prerequisite for Range Sum Query Mutable (56), Count of Smaller Numbers (60), Count of Smaller Numbers After Self (254). The i&(-i) trick isolates the lowest set bit — the key insight.

problems.title: Segment Tree (Build, Point Update, Range Query)
problems.note: Build in O(n). Point update O(log n). Range query O(log n). Handles general associative operations (min, max, gcd, sum). Prerequisite for Range Frequency Queries (77), Range Module (193), Sliding Window Median (160). More powerful than BIT but harder to implement — know when each is needed.

problems.title: Range Sum Query (Mutable)
problems.note: Fenwick Tree (BIT): update O(log n), range sum O(log n). Segment Tree: same complexity but handles general queries (min, max, gcd). Prerequisite for Range Sum Query 2D Mutable (217), Count of Smaller Numbers (60), Range Frequency (150).

problems.title: Count of Smaller Numbers
problems.note: Process right to left. For each element, query BIT for count of elements smaller. Requires coordinate compression. O(n log n). Or merge sort inversion count approach. Databricks data ranking question. Prerequisite for The Skyline Problem (255).

problems.title: Range Frequency Queries
problems.note: HashMap: value → sorted list of indices. Binary search for left and right bounds in the list. O(log n) per query, O(n) preprocessing. Or segment tree with frequency maps. The sorted-indices + binary search approach is typically sufficient.

problems.title: Range Frequency
problems.note: HashMap: value → sorted list of indices. Binary search for leftBound and rightBound indices. O(log n) per query. Same approach as Range Frequency Queries (77). The sorted-index list + binary search is the standard offline approach.

problems.title: Range Module
problems.note: TreeMap of disjoint intervals. addRange: merge all overlapping intervals. removeRange: split/trim overlapping. queryRange: check if fully covered. O(n log n) for each operation worst case. Prerequisite for Disjoint Interval Stream (156). The sorted-interval merging handles all three operations.

problems.title: Construct Pre/Inorder
problems.note: Pre-order first element = root. Find root in in-order to split left/right subtrees. HashMap for O(1) index lookup. O(n). Prerequisite for Construct from Post/Inorder. The inorder hashmap eliminates the O(n) linear search per node.

problems.title: Binary Tree Zigzag
problems.note: BFS level-order + alternate reverse every other level. Or deque: add to front/back based on level parity. O(n). Builds on Level Order (64). The alternate-direction pattern appears in all level-based alternating problems.

problems.title: Boundary of Binary Tree
problems.note: Three phases: left boundary (top-down, exclude leaves), leaves (all leaves left-to-right), right boundary (bottom-up, exclude leaves). O(n). Key: careful not to double-count root and leaf nodes.

problems.title: Maximum Width of Tree
problems.note: BFS with node indices: root=1, left child=2i, right child=2i+1. Width = last_index - first_index + 1. Normalize indices at each level to prevent overflow. O(n). The index normalization (subtract level's first index) is critical for large trees.

problems.title: Recover BST
problems.note: In-order traversal of a BST is sorted. Find two nodes that are out of order. Swap their values. Morris traversal for O(1) space. Two cases: adjacent swap (one inversion) vs non-adjacent (two inversions). The second case is the tricky one.

problems.title: Delete Node in BST
problems.note: Three cases: leaf (remove), one child (replace with child), two children (replace with in-order successor). Find successor as leftmost node of right subtree. O(h). Prerequisite for Recover BST (70). The two-children case is the tricky one.

problems.title: Binary Tree Cameras
problems.note: Greedy: place cameras at leaves' parents, not at leaves. Post-order DFS with 3 states per node: 0=not covered, 1=covered (no camera), 2=has camera. If child is 0, parent needs camera. O(n). The leaf-avoidance greedy minimizes cameras.

problems.title: LCA of N-ary Tree
problems.note: Same as binary tree LCA (55) but with N children. DFS returning node if found. Or binary lifting for O(log n) per query after O(n log n) preprocessing. Prerequisite for LCA IV (94) and all multi-node LCA problems.

problems.title: Lowest Common Ancestor IV
problems.note: Given a set of nodes, find their LCA. DFS: count how many target nodes found in subtree. When count matches a target count and node is in target set, return current node. Or iteratively find LCA of pairs. Builds on LCA (55).

problems.title: House Robber III
problems.note: Post-order DP: for each node return (rob_this, skip_this). rob_this = node.val + skip_left + skip_right. skip_this = max(rob_left, skip_left) + max(rob_right, skip_right). O(n). Extends House Robber (178) from array to tree. Teaches tree state-machine DP.

problems.title: Unique Binary Search Trees
problems.note: Catalan number: C(n) = sum over i of C(i-1)*C(n-i). Bottom-up DP. O(n²). The count = nth Catalan number = C(2n,n)/(n+1). For structure generation (not just count): backtrack over all root choices. Prerequisite for all BST counting problems.

problems.title: Minimum Vertex Cover
problems.note: On trees: DP with states (covered, not covered). Tree DP: dp[node][0] = cover without placing camera, dp[node][1] = cover with camera. Min vertex cover on bipartite graphs = max matching (König's theorem). O(n) for trees.

problems.title: Infection Time in Tree
problems.note: Convert tree to undirected graph (add parent pointers). BFS from the infected node. Time = BFS levels until all nodes visited. O(n). Builds on All Nodes Distance K (13). The tree-to-graph conversion + multi-directional BFS is the universal pattern for 'spread' problems.

problems.title: Symmetric Tree
problems.note: Recursive: check if left subtree is mirror of right. isMirror(left, right): values equal, isMirror(left.left, right.right), isMirror(left.right, right.left). Or BFS with mirrored queue pairs. Builds on Same Tree (91) mirror variant.

problems.title: Subtree of Another Tree
problems.note: For each node in main tree, check Same Tree. O(m·n) naive. Serialize to strings and use KMP or Rabin-Karp for O(m+n). The string matching optimization is the follow-up interviewers ask for.

problems.title: Leaf-Similar Trees
problems.note: DFS to collect leaf sequences from both trees. Compare sequences. O(n+m). Or use generators to yield leaves lazily and compare in lockstep — O(1) extra space. The lazy generator approach is the follow-up interviewers ask for.

problems.title: Balanced Tree Audit
problems.note: Same as Balanced Binary Tree (71) with sentinel -1 optimization. Post-order: return -1 if any subtree is unbalanced. O(n). The key insight is collapsing the isBalanced check and height calculation into one DFS pass.

problems.title: LFU Cache
problems.note: Three structures: key→value map, key→frequency map, frequency→LinkedHashSet of keys. Track min_frequency. On access, move key from freq to freq+1 bucket. O(1) all operations. Harder than LRU (101) — requires the min_frequency tracker and double-keyed maps.

problems.title: Median from Data Stream
problems.note: Max-heap (lower half) + min-heap (upper half). Keep sizes balanced: |low| - |high| ≤ 1. Median = low.peek() or average of both tops. O(log n) insert, O(1) median. Prerequisite for Sliding Window Median (160). The balance-on-insert is the key invariant.

problems.title: Merge K Sorted Lists
problems.note: Min-heap of (value, list_index, node_ptr). Extract min, advance that list's pointer, re-insert. O(n log k). Or divide-and-conquer merge pairs. O(n log k) either way. Prerequisite for Smallest Range Covering K Lists (197). Builds on Merge Two Sorted Lists (268).

problems.title: Single-Threaded CPU
problems.note: Sort tasks by enqueueTime. Simulate time: when CPU free, fast-forward to next available task. Min-heap of (processing_time, index) for available tasks. O(n log n). Teaches event-driven simulation — advance time to next event when idle.

problems.title: Sliding Window Median
problems.note: Max-heap (lower half) + min-heap (upper half). On window slide: remove outgoing element (lazy deletion with a count map), add incoming, rebalance. O(n log k). Extends Median from Data Stream (105) with element removal support.

problems.title: Smallest Range Covering K
problems.note: Min-heap of (value, list_index, element_index). Track current max. Range = [heap_min, current_max]. Advance the list with the minimum element. O(n log k). The key invariant: always have exactly one element per list in the heap.

problems.title: Design Twitter
problems.note: HashMap: user→set of followees, user→list of (timestamp, tweetId). For getNewsFeed: collect all tweets from followees + self, use min-heap to get top 10. O(n log 10) per feed request. Classic fan-out-on-read system design implementation.

problems.title: Design Parking Lot
problems.note: OO design: ParkingLot, Level, ParkingSpot classes. Use TreeSet/PriorityQueue for available spots. Support multiple vehicle sizes. O(log n) park/unpark. Tests OOP hierarchy and resource allocation. Prerequisite for Design File System (113) structurally.

problems.title: Design Spreadsheet
problems.note: Topological sort for cell evaluation order. DFS cycle detection for circular references. Propagate updates via reverse dependency graph. O(V+E) per update. Combines DAG + topo sort + reactive computation. Classic spreadsheet engine design.

problems.title: Distributed ID Generator
problems.note: Snowflake-style: 41-bit timestamp + 10-bit machine ID + 12-bit sequence. Clock skew handling: wait or use last timestamp. O(1) generation. Databricks distributed systems question. The bit-packing layout and clock-monotonicity guarantee are the key design decisions.

problems.title: Distributed Rate Limiter
problems.note: Token bucket: refill at rate r, burst up to capacity b. Redis-based distributed: Lua script for atomic check-and-decrement. Sliding window log: deque of timestamps. Uber API gateway design. Token bucket handles bursty traffic; sliding window is smoother.

problems.title: Sliding Window Max
problems.note: Monotonic decreasing deque. Add to back (remove all smaller elements first). Front is always the max. Remove front when it leaves window. O(n). Prerequisite for Sliding Window Median (160) and Stock Price Fluctuation (198). The deque maintains a decreasing invariant.

problems.title: Trapping Rain Water
problems.note: Two pointers from both ends. Track left_max and right_max. Water at position i = min(left_max, right_max) - height[i]. O(n) time, O(1) space. Or monotonic stack approach. Prerequisite for Largest Histogram Rectangle (129) conceptually.

problems.title: Largest Hist. Rectangle
problems.note: Monotonic increasing stack. When height decreases, pop and compute area using current index as right boundary. O(n). Prerequisite for Maximal Rectangle (130). The sentinel 0 at the end forces all bars to be processed.

problems.title: Maximal Rectangle
problems.note: Build histogram row by row. For each row apply Largest Rectangle in Histogram (129). O(m·n). The reduction to histogram is the key insight — each column height = consecutive 1s ending at current row.

problems.title: Sparse Matrix Multiply
problems.note: Skip zero elements. For each non-zero in A[i][k], iterate non-zeros in B[k][j]. O(nnz_A × cols_B) vs O(m·k·n) dense. Or CSR format. Databricks sparse data processing interview problem. The skip-zero optimization is the core insight.

problems.title: Valid Sudoku
problems.note: Three sets of HashSets: rows[9], cols[9], boxes[9]. Box index = (row/3)*3 + col/3. Single pass O(81). No solving, just validation. Prerequisite for Solve Sudoku (backtracking). The box indexing formula is the key trick.

problems.title: Smallest Subarray (K)
problems.note: Sliding window with frequency map and distinct count tracker. Shrink from left when all K distinct chars are present. O(n). Builds on Find All Anagrams (141). The distinct-count variable avoids re-scanning the frequency map each step.

problems.title: Longest Substring (K)
problems.note: Sliding window with HashMap of char counts and distinct counter. When distinct > K, shrink left. O(n). Prerequisite for Minimum Window Substring (187). The distinct-count variable is the key shortcut over scanning the map each iteration.

problems.title: Repeating Char Replace
problems.note: Sliding window tracking max frequency of any char. Valid window: length - max_freq ≤ k. Expand right; if invalid, slide left. O(n). Key insight: max_freq never decreases — window never shrinks below best seen size.

problems.title: Min Window Substring
problems.note: Sliding window with frequency map and 'formed' counter. Shrink when all required chars are covered. O(n). Prerequisite for all minimum-window problems. The 'formed' count avoids scanning the entire map on each shrink.

problems.title: Longest Consecutive Seq.
problems.note: HashSet of all numbers. For each n where n-1 is NOT in set (start of sequence), count consecutive. O(n). Or DSU union consecutive pairs. The start-of-sequence check ensures each streak is counted once.

problems.title: First Missing Positive
problems.note: Use array as hash: place each number i at index i-1 if in range [1,n]. Scan for first position where arr[i] ≠ i+1. O(n) time, O(1) space. The in-place swap (cyclic sort) turns the array into a presence map.

problems.title: Find Duplicate Number
problems.note: Treat array as linked list: index i points to arr[i]. Duplicate creates a cycle — apply Floyd's cycle detection. O(n) time, O(1) space. No modification of array. Prerequisite requires Linked List Cycle Detection (270) pattern.

problems.title: Majority Element II
problems.note: Extended Boyer-Moore: maintain two candidates. After pass, verify both actually exceed n/3. At most 2 such elements can exist. Teaches the generalization from n/2 majority to n/k majority — requires k-1 candidates.

problems.title: Snapshot Array
problems.note: Each index stores list of (snap_id, value). On get(index, snap_id): binary search for largest snap_id ≤ given snap_id. O(log s) per get. Prerequisite for Time Based KV Store (125). The sparse version storage avoids O(n) snapshot cost.

problems.title: Time Based KV Store
problems.note: HashMap: key → sorted list of (timestamp, value). Binary search for largest timestamp ≤ given time. O(log n) get. Prerequisite for Stock Price Fluctuation (198). The sorted-by-timestamp + binary-search pattern is universal for temporal queries.

problems.title: Product Except Self
problems.note: Left pass: prefix products. Right pass: suffix products. Multiply in place. O(n) time, O(1) extra space (output array doesn't count). Prerequisite for Range Sum Query 2D (135). The two-pass prefix-suffix product pattern generalizes to many aggregation problems.

problems.title: Continuous Subarray Sum
problems.note: Track prefix_sum % k in HashMap. If same remainder seen before at index j, subarray (j+1, i) has sum divisible by k. Need length ≥ 2: store earliest index for each remainder. O(n). Builds on Subarray Sum Equals K (133).

problems.title: Range Sum Query 2D
problems.note: Build 2D prefix sum. Query (r1,c1,r2,c2) = P[r2][c2] - P[r1-1][c2] - P[r2][c1-1] + P[r1-1][c1-1]. O(1) query after O(m·n) build. Prerequisite for Range Sum 2D Mutable (217) and Number of Ways to Cut Pizza (230). Inclusion-exclusion is the formula.

problems.title: Design Food Rating
problems.note: Two HashMaps: food → category and food → rating. One category → TreeSet of (rating, name). TreeSet keeps elements sorted for O(log n) update and O(1) query. Prerequisite for Movie Rental System (137). The multi-map + sorted set pattern is standard for leaderboard designs.
